content: The Sharpe ratio measures risk-adjusted return: (portfolio return - risk-free rate) / portfolio standard deviation. A Sharpe of 1 means one unit of excess return per unit of volatility. Higher ratios indicate better risk-adjusted performance. Developed by William Sharpe (1966), it enables comparison across strategies with different risk profiles. Limitations include: assumes normal distributions (understates tail risk), treats upside and downside volatility equally, and is sensitive to the time period measured. For non-normal returns, alternatives like Sortino ratio (downside deviation only) or Calmar ratio (return/max drawdown) may be more appropriate. Hedge funds typically target Sharpe ratios above 1; passive equity indices average 0.3-0.5 over long periods.
